---
title: "Chapter 4: Data Transformation with dplyr"
subtitle: "R for Management Consultants | BK Advisors"
author: "BK Advisors"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    logo: bka-logo.png
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: slide
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#005CB9"
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

::: {.incremental}
- Use the **pipe operator** (`|>`) to chain operations into readable workflows
- **Filter** rows based on conditions with `filter()`
- **Select** and rename columns with `select()` and `rename()`
- **Create new columns** with `mutate()` and `case_when()`
- **Summarize** data by groups with `summarise()` and `group_by()`
- **Sort** results with `arrange()`
:::


## Chapter Overview

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #E24A3F;">The<br>Pipe</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;">filter()</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;">select()</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #FA7650;">mutate()</div>
<div class="step-arrow">&rarr;</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;">summarise() +<br>group_by()</div>
</div>


# The Pipe Operator {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Chaining operations into readable workflows</span>


## The Pipe: `|>`

::: {.definition-box}
The **pipe operator** `|>` takes the result of one function and passes it as the first argument to the next. It reads like a sentence: "Take this, then do that, then do that."
:::

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.bad-example}
[Without pipes: nested, inside-out]{.example-label}

```{r}
#| label: pipe-bad
#| echo: true

# Hard to read — you must start
# from the innermost function
round(mean(facility_data$nmr,
           na.rm = TRUE), 1)
```
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.good-example}
[With pipes: step by step, left to right]{.example-label}

```{r}
#| label: pipe-good
#| echo: true

# Reads top to bottom, like English
facility_data$nmr |>
  mean(na.rm = TRUE) |>
  round(1)
```
:::
:::
:::


## Pipe Workflows

```{r}
#| label: pipe-workflow
#| echo: true

library(dplyr)

# A typical analysis workflow reads like English:
facility_data |>                           # Start with data
  filter(country == "Ethiopia") |>         # Keep Ethiopian facilities
  filter(!is.na(nmr)) |>                   # Remove missing NMR
  summarise(                               # Calculate summary stats
    n_facilities = n(),
    avg_nmr = mean(nmr),
    avg_deliveries = mean(delivery_volume)
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Read the pipe as "and then."** Take facility_data, AND THEN filter to Ethiopia, AND THEN remove missing NMR, AND THEN summarize.
:::


# filter() --- Subset Rows {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Keeping only the rows you need</span>


## filter() Basics

```{r}
#| label: filter-basic
#| echo: true

library(dplyr)

# Facilities in Ethiopia
facility_data |>
  filter(country == "Ethiopia")

# Facilities with NMR above 30 (critical)
facility_data |>
  filter(nmr > 30)

# Hospitals only
facility_data |>
  filter(facility_type == "Hospital")
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**`filter()` keeps rows where the condition is `TRUE` and drops everything else.** Think of it as a sieve --- only rows that pass the test come through.
:::


## Combining Conditions

```{r}
#| label: filter-combine
#| echo: true

# AND: both conditions must be true (use & or comma)
facility_data |>
  filter(country == "Tanzania", nmr > 25)

# OR: at least one condition true
facility_data |>
  filter(country == "Ethiopia" | country == "Kenya")

# IN: match any value in a list
facility_data |>
  filter(country %in% c("Ethiopia", "Kenya", "Malawi"))

# NOT: exclude
facility_data |>
  filter(facility_type != "Health Post")
```


## Practical Filters

```{r}
#| label: filter-practical
#| echo: true

# High-risk facilities: high NMR + no CPAP
facility_data |>
  filter(nmr > 30, has_cpap == FALSE)

# Active facilities with sufficient data
facility_data |>
  filter(delivery_volume >= 20, !is.na(nmr))

# Facilities that completed training
facility_data |>
  filter(training_completed == TRUE)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Always check for missing values.** `filter(nmr > 30)` silently drops rows where `nmr` is `NA`. Use `!is.na()` explicitly when you want to keep or remove missing data.
:::


# select() --- Pick Columns {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Choosing the variables you need</span>


## select() Basics

```{r}
#| label: select-basic
#| echo: true

# Keep specific columns
facility_data |>
  select(facility_id, country, nmr, delivery_volume)

# Remove columns (use minus sign)
facility_data |>
  select(-has_ultrasound, -region)

# Select a range
facility_data |>
  select(facility_id:facility_type)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: select-helpers
#| echo: true

# Helper functions — powerful shortcuts
facility_data |>
  select(starts_with("has_"))   # has_cpap, has_ultrasound

facility_data |>
  select(where(is.numeric))     # all numeric columns
```


## rename() --- Better Column Names

```{r}
#| label: rename
#| echo: true

# Rename specific columns: new_name = old_name
facility_data |>
  rename(
    neonatal_mortality = nmr,
    maternal_mortality = mmr,
    facility = facility_id
  )

# Clean messy DHIS2 column names
# janitor::clean_names() is useful for this (not covered in detail)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**DHIS2 exports often have column names with spaces, dots, or inconsistent casing.** `rename()` lets you fix these one at a time; `janitor::clean_names()` fixes them all at once.
:::


# mutate() --- Create New Columns {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Computing new variables from existing ones</span>


## mutate() Basics

```{r}
#| label: mutate-basic
#| echo: true

facility_data |>
  mutate(
    # Calculate stillbirth rate from existing columns
    total_births = delivery_volume,

    # Convert coverage from proportion to percentage
    anc_pct = anc_coverage * 100,

    # Flag high-risk facilities
    high_risk = nmr > 30
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**`mutate()` adds new columns while keeping all existing ones.** Each new column can reference columns you just created in the same `mutate()` call.
:::


## case_when() --- Conditional Logic

```{r}
#| label: case-when
#| echo: true

facility_data |>
  mutate(
    # Classify NMR into categories
    nmr_category = case_when(
      nmr <= 12            ~ "On Track (SDG Target)",
      nmr <= 25            ~ "Needs Improvement",
      nmr <= 40            ~ "High Risk",
      nmr > 40             ~ "Critical",
      TRUE                 ~ "Unknown"
    ),

    # Classify facility size
    size = case_when(
      delivery_volume >= 150 ~ "High Volume",
      delivery_volume >= 50  ~ "Medium Volume",
      TRUE                   ~ "Low Volume"
    )
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**`case_when()` is R's version of nested IF statements in Excel, but cleaner.** Conditions are evaluated top to bottom --- the first match wins.
:::


## Practical mutate() Examples

```{r}
#| label: mutate-practical
#| echo: true

facility_data |>
  mutate(
    # Equipment readiness score (0-2)
    equip_score = as.integer(has_cpap) + as.integer(has_ultrasound),

    # ANC coverage gap (distance from 80% target)
    anc_gap = pmax(0.80 - anc_coverage, 0),

    # Combined risk flag
    priority = (nmr > 25) & (staffing_level == "Low")
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**In MNH analysis, `mutate()` is where you turn raw data into decision-ready indicators.** Coverage gaps, risk flags, readiness scores --- these are the columns that drive recommendations.
:::


# summarise() + group_by() --- Aggregate Data {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">From raw data to insights</span>


## summarise() Basics

```{r}
#| label: summarise-basic
#| echo: true

facility_data |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    avg_nmr = mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_nmr = median(nmr, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_deliveries = mean(delivery_volume, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_deliveries = sum(delivery_volume, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_with_cpap = mean(has_cpap, na.rm = TRUE) * 100
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**`summarise()` collapses many rows into a single summary row.** Without `group_by()`, it summarizes the entire dataset at once.
:::


## group_by() + summarise() --- The Power Combination

```{r}
#| label: group-summarise
#| echo: true

# Summary by country
facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    n_facilities = n(),
    avg_nmr = round(mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    avg_deliveries = round(mean(delivery_volume, na.rm = TRUE), 0),
    pct_trained = round(mean(training_completed, na.rm = TRUE) * 100, 1)
  )
```

. . .

```{r}
#| label: group-summarise-two
#| echo: true

# Summary by country AND facility type
facility_data |>
  group_by(country, facility_type) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    avg_nmr = mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**`group_by() |> summarise()` is the R equivalent of a pivot table in Excel.** It's the most important pattern in dplyr --- you'll use it in almost every analysis.
:::


## arrange() --- Sort Results

```{r}
#| label: arrange
#| echo: true

# Sort by NMR (lowest first — best performing)
facility_data |>
  arrange(nmr)

# Sort by NMR descending (highest first — worst performing)
facility_data |>
  arrange(desc(nmr))

# Sort by country, then by NMR within country
facility_data |>
  arrange(country, desc(nmr))
```


## count() --- Quick Frequency Tables

```{r}
#| label: count
#| echo: true

# How many facilities per country?
facility_data |>
  count(country)

# How many per country and facility type?
facility_data |>
  count(country, facility_type)

# Sort by frequency
facility_data |>
  count(country, sort = TRUE)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**`count()` is a shortcut for `group_by() |> summarise(n = n())`.** Use it whenever you need a quick frequency table.
:::


## Putting It All Together

```{r}
#| label: full-pipeline
#| echo: true

# Country-level MNH dashboard summary
facility_data |>
  filter(!is.na(nmr), delivery_volume >= 10) |>
  mutate(
    nmr_status = case_when(
      nmr <= 12 ~ "On Track",
      nmr <= 25 ~ "Progress",
      TRUE      ~ "Off Track"
    )
  ) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    facilities = n(),
    avg_nmr = round(mean(nmr), 1),
    pct_on_track = round(mean(nmr_status == "On Track") * 100, 1),
    total_deliveries = sum(delivery_volume)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(avg_nmr))
```

. . .

::: {.key-takeaway}
**This single pipeline replaces dozens of Excel steps:** filtering, calculating, categorizing, grouping, and sorting --- all in one readable chain.
:::


## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaway}
**The pipe (`|>`) chains operations into readable workflows** that replace nested functions and manual Excel steps.

**`filter()`, `select()`, `mutate()`, `summarise()`, and `group_by()` are the five dplyr verbs** that handle 90% of data transformation tasks.

**`group_by() |> summarise()` is your pivot table replacement** --- learn it well.
:::


## Looking Ahead

::: {.callout-box}
**Next: Chapter 5 --- Reshaping & Joining Datasets**

- `pivot_longer()` and `pivot_wider()` --- switching between long and wide formats
- Joining datasets: linking facility data with country indicators
- String manipulation basics with `stringr`
:::


##  {background-color="white"}

<br><br><br>

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![](bka-logo.png){width="250px"}

<br>

[R for Management Consultants | Chapter 4: Data Transformation with dplyr]{style="color: #005CB9; font-size: 0.6em;"}

[BK Advisors]{style="color: #666; font-size: 0.5em;"}
:::
